coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive transglutaminase based nanosensor for early detection of celiac disease in human
coredata.dc:creator: Gupta, Shagun
Kaushal, Ankur
Kumar, Ashok
Kumar, Dinesh
coredata.dc:description: A GQD/PAMAM nanohybrid modified on gold nanoparticles (AuNP) embedded in multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) based immunosensor was developed for detection of celiac disease in human. The AuNP embedded in MWCNT were linked with thiol groups of cysteine and amino groups were further coupled with carboxyl moieties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) using EDC-NHS cross-linking chemistry. To enhance the sensitivity of the sensor, polyamidoamine (PAMAM) dendrimer was attached with GQDs through carbodiimide coupling. The tissue transglutaminase (tTG) antigen was immobilized on PAMAM to form nanoprobe for sensing specific anti-tissue transglutaminase (anti-tTG) antibodies (immunoglobin A isotype) in human serum. The electrochemical changes after specific binding of antibody with antigen was measured cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). The modifications in sensor surface were characterized using FE-SEM. The sensor is highly specific to anti-tissue transglutaminase antibodies and showed negligible response to non-specific serum proteins. The sensitivity of the sensor was 1297.14μA/cm2/pg with DPV and lower limit of detection of anti-tissue transglutaminase antibody was found 0.1fg per 6μl.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Anti-tissue transglutaminase
Celiac disease
Celiac sensor
Immunosensor
Tissue transglutaminase

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of GQD-PAMAM nanohybrid modified MWCNT-AuNP electrode
Electrochemical detection of anti-tTG on GQD-PAMAM nanohybrid modified electrode
Specificity, stability and accuracy of the sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of immobilization of tissue transglutaminase antigen (tTG) on MWCNT-AuNP/GQD/PAMAM nanohybrid electrode and electrochemical detection of anti-tTG antibodies. [SPE: Screen printed electrode, W: working (multi-walled carbon nanotubes-gold nanoparticles) electrode, R: reference (silver) electrode, C: counter (carbon) electrode, GQD: graphene quantum dots, PAMAM: poly(amidoamine), tTG: tissue transglutaminase (antigen), ant-tTG: anti-tissue transglutaminase (antibody)].

Cyclic voltammetry studies of MWCNT-AuNP/GQD/PAMAM/tTG and immune-binding with 10−4–104 pg/6μl anti-tTG antibody in serum (a-j) using 5mMK3[Fe(CN)6] in 0.1M PBS, pH 7.4. Inset [A] shows hyperbolic curve between relative Ip (with respect to control) and increasing concentrations of anti-tTG antibodies ranging from 10−4–104 pg/6μl (0.1fg–10ng/6μl) in serum. Inset [B] shows the linear plot from 10−4-10−1 pg/6μl(0.1fg-100fg/6μl) anti-tTG antibodies for the calculation of sensitivity and LOD.

Differential pulse voltammetry studies of MWCNT-AuNP/GQD/PAMAM/tTG and immune-binding with 10−4–104 pg/6μl anti-tTG antibody in serum (a-j) using 5mMK3[Fe(CN)6] in 0.1M PBS, pH 7.4. Inset [A] shows hyperbolic curve between relative Ip (with respect to control) and increasing concentrations of anti-tTG antibodies ranging from 10−4–104 pg/6μl in serum. Inset [B] shows the linear plot from 10−4- 10−1 pg/6μl (0.1fg-100fg/6μl) anti-tTG antibodies for sensitivity and LOD calculation.
FE-SEM images of (A) MWCNT-AuNP/Cysteine (B) MWCNT-AuNP/Cysteine/GQD (C) MWCNT-AuNP/Cysteine/GQD/PAMAM (D) MWCNT-AuNP/Cysteine/GQD/PAMAM/tTG and (E) MWCNT-AuNP/Cysteine/GQD/PAMAM/tTG/anti-tTG.

The specificity of MWCNT-AuNP/Cysteine/GQD/PAMAM/tTG sensor with anti-tTG antibody and other non-specific serum proteins. The bar graphs shows [A] the average relative decrease Ip values and [B] Percent relative decrease Ip values of DPV (with respect to the tTG antigen) against specific and non-specific serum proteins (10pg/6μl).
Comparison of different modified electrodes for detection of celiac disease in human.

row.entry: Gold-based self-assembled monolayers of a carboxylic group terminated bipodal alkanethiol
Dual SPCE nanostructured with a carbon-gold hybrid system
Glassy carbon electrode functionalized with gold nanoparticles
Disulphide modified tTG self assembled on gold surface

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive transglutaminase based nanosensor for early detection of celiac disease in human

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shoolini University, Post Box No. 9, Head Post Office, Solan, H.P. 173212, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CSIR-Institute of Genomics and Integrative Biology, Mall Road, Delhi-110007, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CSIR-Institute of Genomics and Integrative Biology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A GQD/PAMAM nanohybrid modified on gold nanoparticles (AuNP) embedded in multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) based immunosensor was developed for detection of celiac disease in human. The AuNP embedded in MWCNT were linked with thiol groups of cysteine and amino groups were further coupled with carboxyl moieties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) using EDC-NHS cross-linking chemistry. To enhance the sensitivity of the sensor, polyamidoamine (PAMAM) dendrimer was attached with GQDs through carbodiimide coupling. The tissue transglutaminase (tTG) antigen was immobilized on PAMAM to form nanoprobe for sensing specific anti-tissue transglutaminase (anti-tTG) antibodies (immunoglobin A isotype) in human serum. The electrochemical changes after specific binding of antibody with antigen was measured cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). The modifications in sensor surface were characterized using FE-SEM. The sensor is highly specific to anti-tissue transglutaminase antibodies and showed negligible response to non-specific serum proteins. The sensitivity of the sensor was 1297.14μA/cm2/pg with DPV and lower limit of detection of anti-tissue transglutaminase antibody was found 0.1fg per 6μl.

ce:section.ce:para: Increasing evidence is accumulating for a steady rise in the frequency of autoimmune diseases (AD) in the last decades [1]. Autoimmune disorders include predominantly celiac disease (CD), multiple sclerosis (MS), autoimmune thyroiditis (AT), type 1 diabetes (IDDM, insulin dependent diabetes mellitus), inflammatory bowel diseases (mainly Crohn’s disease) (IBD), systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), primary biliary cirrhosis, hepatitis and rheumatic diseases (RA) [2,3]. The challenging diagnosis of CD due to its wide variety of clinical symptoms has drawn great attention by researchers. CD is an autoimmune disorder of small intestine triggered by dietary gluten in genetically susceptible individuals [4]. CD patients, experience immune response and the body produces auto-antibodies causing the destruction of intestinal mucous [5]. Intestinal biopsy is considered as a gold standard for the diagnosis of CD [6]. Common serological changes of this condition include the appearance of antibodies against gliadin (AGA) and anti-tissue transglutaminase (anti-tTG) antibodies; which are specific serological markers of the disease [5]. The immunoglobulin A (IgA) isotype is considered to be the most specific however, selective IgA deficiency affects about 2–5% of patients diagnosed with CD [7]. In these cases, the determination of the IgG class of antibodies is considered [8].

The most common method for detection of CD biomarkers is enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [9,10]. However, the method suffers from various limitations of being less sensitive, tedious, slow, expensive and require sophisticated equipment. Therefore, biosensors have turn out to be more acceptable due to its several advantages viz., an increase in sensitivity due to the electrochemical detection, lower sample volumes and require shorter time of analysis [11–13–15].Various detection methods have been reported including impedimetric, colorimetric, chemi-resistive and electrochemical based methods [16–19]. Among these detection techniques, electrochemical method is simple, rapid and sensitive which are broadly used to detect various biological molecules [20–23].

Recently, nano materials based strategies used to develop different biosensors to improve the sensitivity and specificity. In this regard, graphene quantum dots (GQD) which are planar nanocrystals of graphene with less than 100nm in size with higher surface to volume ratio and from single to multi-layers, have prompted interest in optical, biosensing as well as in diagnostic applications [24–26]. Similarly, dendrimers poly(amidoamine) are used in electrochemical biosensors due to their branched (tree-like) polymeric structures that contain large number of amino groups for conjugation with desired carboxylated probes [27]. Thus, the exceptional features of GQD and PAMAM may be combined and further explored to develop a diagnostic tool that lead to successful detection and quantification of target analyte.

Various electrochemical sensors have been developed for the diagnosis of celiac disease [13–15,28–31]. Although, the reported sensors performed well in terms of sensitivity but lower limit of detecting the antibodies raised in response to gluten ingestion were found higher. Therefore, in the present study an electrochemical sensor was developed for detection of anti-tissue transglutaminase antibodies using GQD/PAMAM nanohybrid modified AuNP embedded in MWCNT screen printed electrode with immobilized tTG. The working electrode (MWCNT-AuNP) was functionalized with cysteine which acts as a linker between Au and GQD. The GQD was further attached with dendrimer PAMAM through carbodiimide bond. Several tTG antigenic probes are covalently linked with PAMAM through EDC/NHS chemistry. The anti-tTG antibodies are captured on the above modified MWCNT-AuNP/GQD/PAMAM/tTG electrode through antigen-antibody interaction. The electrochemical response was recorded in the form of cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). The sensor was further tested with various non-specific antigens released in human blood at different physiological states. The proposed sensor was also validated with present available methods and results were compared in terms of specificity, sensitivity and limit of detection of anti-tTG antibody.

Tissue transglutaminase antigen (tTG) was purchased from International scientific and surgicals, India, Polyamidoamine (PAMAM) dendrimer fourth generation (G4; Mol. Wt.-14215), cysteine, 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Graphene quantum dots (GQD) obtained from ACS material, USA. Potassium ferricyanide [K3Fe(CN6)], sodium chloride, potassium chloride di-sodium hydrogen orthophosphate (Na2HPO4), sodium dihydrogen ortho-phosphate (NaH2PO4) and other chemicals were obtained from Qualigens, India. All other reagents were analytical grade and solutions were prepared in deionized Milli Q water. Multi-walled carbon nanotubes-gold nanoparticles modified screen printed carbon electrodes (DS110 CNT-GNP) were procured from DropSens, Spain and modified at IGIB, Delhi. Tissue transglutaminase ELISA kit was purchased from Diagnostics and Scientific Suppliers, Chandigarh, India. The human serum samples were obtained from Health Centre, Shoolini University, Solan (HP).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of GQD-PAMAM nanohybrid modified MWCNT-AuNP electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The multi-walled carbon nanotubes-gold nanoparticles modified screen printed electrode (consist of MWCNT-AuNP as the working electrode, carbon as the counter electrode and silver as the reference electrode) was modified for the fabrication of GQD/PAMAM nanohybrid sensor. The working MWCNT-AuNP electrode surface (0.126cm2) was incubated with 6μl cysteine (5mM in Milli-Q water) overnight at 4°C for formation of S-Au bond between AuNP and cysteine followed by subsequent 3–4 times washing with Milli-Q water to remove unbound cysteine and dried at room temperature (25°C). Further 5μl equimolar mixture of 10mM EDC and NHS (1:1, v/v) in PBS (100mM sodium phosphate buffer, pH 7.4 containing 0.9% chloride) was added in 10μl GQD solution (100μgml−1) to convert the carboxyl groups into active reactive ester. Then, 6μl of the above solution was placed on the working electrode surface for 3h to allow the formation of amide bond between −COOH groups of the GQD and −NH2 groups of cysteine. The electrode was then washed 3–4 times as earlier to remove unbound GQD followed by addition of 6μl of PAMAM dendrimer solution (165μgml−1) to form MWCNT-AuNP/GQD/PAMAM nanohybrid electrode.

The carboxyl terminal of the antigen was activated by mixing 10μl tTG solution (2mgml−1) with 5μl of equimolar mixture of 10mM EDC and NHS (1:1, v/v) to make the final probe concentration 1mgml−1. Then, 6μl of the above reaction mixture was incubated for 3h at 4°C onto the MWCNT-AuNP/GQD/PAMAM working electrode to allow the formation of the amide bond between the −COOH groups of tTG and −NH2 groups of PAMAM to form MWCNT-AuNP/GQD/PAMAM/tTG nanohybrid electrode. The unbound antigen (tTG) was removed by washing 4–5 times with PBS, pH 7.4 and dried at room temperature before electrochemical measurements.

The electrochemical response of the electrode was studied with CV and DPV using redox 5mMK3[Fe(CN)6] solution in PBS, pH 7.4. The MWCNT-AuNP/GQD/PAMAM/tTG working electrode was initially incubated with 6μl human blood serum (as control) for 10min followed by washing and subsequent electrochemical detection as CV and DPV. Further, similar measurements were carried out with different concentrations of anti-tTG prepared in blood serum from 10−4 pg to104 pg (0.1fg–10ng) per 6μl. To ensure the specificity of the sensor, non-specific antigens or proteins released in human blood at different physiological conditions were also explored.

The surface topology of the developed sensor at different stages of fabrication viz. MWCNT-AuNP (control), MWCNT-AuNP/GQD, MWCNT-AuNP/GQD/PAMAM, MWCNT-AuNP/GQD/PAMAM/tTG and MWCNT-AuNP/GQD/PAMAM/tTG/anti-tTG was characterized using Field emission-scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM).

The schematic assembly of the MWCNT-AuNP/GQD/PAMAM electrode, immobilization of tTG antigen and binding with anti-tTG antibodies to form MWCNT-AuNP/GQD/PAMAM/tTG/anti-tTG and its electro-chemical detection is represented in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical detection of anti-tTG on GQD-PAMAM nanohybrid modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: In the absence of anti-tTG, the GQD-PAMAM nanohybrid modified electrode immobilized with tTG antigen shows more current response. In contrast, in the presence of anti-tTG, there is a decrease in peak current in CV and DPV with increase in the concentrations of anti-tTG in serum. This was due to the formation of more antigen-antibody complex on working electrode surface causing decrease in redox current.

The CV peak current (Ip) of tTG antigen (MWCNT-AuNP/GQD/PAMAM/tTG) electrode incubated with serum was taken as control (25μA) for various anti-tTG antibody concentrations prepared in human blood serum (Fig. 1 ). The redox Ip of MWCNT-AuNP/GQD/PAMAM/tTG/anti-tTG was lower than that of MWCNT-AuNP/GQD/PAMAM/tTG and it decreases with increasing concentrations of anti-tTG antibody through successive antigen-antibody (immune complex) formation. The plot between the various concentrations of anti-tTG antibody and the relative anodic Ip values with respect to the antigen tTG (control) was hyperbolic (Fig. 1, inset [A]) for anti-tTG antibody following a linear equation [Ip (μA)=55.711(μApg−1)×anti-tTG antibody (pg) + +6.7026] and regression coefficient (R2) 0.9918 (Fig. 1, inset [B]). The sensitivity (S) of the sensor was 442.15μA/cm2 pg−1 which was calculated using the formula, S
=
m/A, where, m is the slope of the linear equation and A is the area of the working (0.126cm2) electrode. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated using the formula LOD=3 (s/S) where, s is the standard deviation and S is the sensitivity [32]. The LOD for anti-tTG antibody was found 0.3 fg per 6μl (50fgml−1).

The nanohybrid electrode was characterized using FE-SEM. The FE-SEM images at different stages modifications of the MWCNT-AuNP composite electrodes are shown in Fig. 3 . The SEM image of the MWCNT-AuNP electrode with cysteine in Fig. 3A exhibits a smooth and uniform structure at magnification of 15000×. Fig. 3B shows the image of MWCNT-AuNP/GQD as multiple sheets like structure of graphene which is different from the previous one on same magnification confirms the presence of GQD on MWCNT-AuNP. Fig. 3C shows small globular polymeric structure covered GQD confirm the attachment of dendrimer PAMAM in the image of MWCNT-AuNP/GQD/PAMAM on 15000× magnification. Fig. 3D image at same magnification is covered with small dense spherical structure suggest the presence of antigen attachment to the dendrimer on MWCNT-AuNP/GQD/PAMAM/tTG modified electrode surface. Fig. 3E shows changes in surface morphology (20,000x) as cloudy cluster covered small globular structure of antigen in MWCNT-AuNP/GQD/PAMAM/tTG/anti-tTG electrode after binding with ant-tTG capture antibody with tTG antigen confirms immune complex formation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity, stability and accuracy of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The accuracy of the present sensor was checked with the already available method ELISA (Fig.S1) for anti-tTG which showed a good correlation (R2) 0.996 between the two methods (Supplementary data). The different concentrations of anti-tTG antibodies in serum were determined by ELISA kit (y-axis) [33] and by the present GQD-PAMAM nanohybrid based sensor (x-axis). The unknown concentrations of anti-tTG antibody (0.8, 4, 8, and 10pg/ml in serum) were made and real values obtained by both methods (ELISA and sensor) were plotted which showed a good correlation (R2 =0.996) with linear equation (y=0.953x) (Fig. S2). The present sensor can be used in point-of-care diagnosis in hospitals and remote areas where sophisticated instruments are not available.

The tTG antigen conjugated with GQD-PAMAM nanohybrid based sensor can detect as low as 0.1 fg per 6μl (20fgml−1) anti-tTG antibody in 10min for Celiac disease diagnosis. The sensitivity of the electrode sensor was 1297.94μAcm−2pg−1 with DPV and found highly specific to only anti-tTG antibody. The advantage of the sensor is that it requires one drop (6μl) of blood to detect the disease only in 20min. The sensor electrode was found stable on storage in freeze at 4°C which can be used for detection of the disease without making fresh electrode.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to Prof. P.K. Khosla, Vice Chancellor, Shoolini University, Solan (H.P) and Molecular Biosensor Lab, CSIR-IGIB, Mall Road, Delhi for providing the facility to carry out the research work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2017.07.126.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

Multiplexed electrochemical immunosensor for detection of celiac disease serological markers
Celiac disease detection using a transglutaminase electrochemical immunosensor fabricated on nanohybrid screen-printed carbon electrodes
